highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.119 Released
highlights.summary: The latest release makes /config settings like theme, editor mode, and verbose output persistent — they now save to ~/.claude/settings.json and respect project/local/policy override precedence. A new prUrlTemplate setting lets teams point the PR badge in the footer to a custom code-review URL instead of GitHub. This is a quality-of-life improvement that means your preferences survive restarts and can be shared across projects.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.118 Released
highlights.summary: This release adds vim visual mode (v) and visual-line mode (V) with full selection and operator support, making Claude Code much friendlier for vim users. The /cost and /stats commands have been merged into a single /usage command, simplifying how you track token and cost metrics. You can now also create and switch between named custom themes directly from /theme.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.116 Released
highlights.summary: Resuming large sessions (40MB+) is now up to 67% faster, which is a big win if you work on long-running projects. MCP startup has also been accelerated when multiple stdio servers are configured, by deferring resource template loading until the first @-mention. These performance improvements make Claude Code noticeably snappier in real-world, heavy-use scenarios.

highlights.title: An Update on Recent Claude Code Quality Reports (April 23 Postmortem)
highlights.summary: Anthropic published a postmortem addressing community reports of degraded Claude Code output quality, explaining what went wrong and the steps taken to fix it. This is worth reading if you noticed unusual or regressed behavior in Claude Code around late April 2026. It also gives insight into how Anthropic monitors and responds to quality regressions in production AI tools.

highlights.title: Introducing Claude Opus 4.7
highlights.summary: Anthropic has announced Claude Opus 4.7, the latest addition to the Opus model family, which is the most capable tier for complex reasoning and coding tasks. For Claude Code users, model upgrades like this can directly improve the quality of code generation, refactoring, and multi-step agentic tasks. Check the Anthropic blog for details on new capabilities and how to select the model within Claude Code.

highlights.title: Mastering Claude Code in 30 Minutes
highlights.summary: This YouTube tutorial promises a fast-paced but comprehensive walkthrough of Claude Code, targeting developers who want to get productive quickly without sitting through a multi-hour course. It's ideal if you've already installed Claude Code but feel like you're only scratching the surface of what it can do. Bookmark this for a focused learning session during a lunch break.

highlights.title: Ask HN: How Are You Using AI Code Assistants on Large Messy Legacy Codebases?
highlights.summary: A senior developer with 20+ years of experience started this Hacker News thread asking how others are applying AI coding tools like Claude to sprawling, undocumented legacy code. The discussion surfaces practical strategies for scoping prompts, managing context limits, and knowing when AI assistance helps versus hurts in brownfield projects. Highly relevant if your day job involves maintaining inherited code rather than greenfield development.

tips_of_the_day: Run /config in Claude Code to review all your current settings, then check ~/.claude/settings.json to confirm they persisted — this file is also safe to commit to a shared dotfiles repo so your team shares a consistent baseline.

If you're working on a legacy codebase, break your context into focused chunks: open only the files directly relevant to the task and use a concise CLAUDE.md file in each subdirectory to describe that module's purpose, reducing the chance Claude loses track of intent across large sessions.

Switch to the new /usage command (replacing /cost and /stats) to get a single unified view of your token consumption and spending — check it at the end of each work session to build intuition for which task types burn the most tokens.
